text: The patient is positioned in lithotomy for the laparoscopic duodenal switch procedure. The surgeon performs a sleeve gastrectomy, mobilizing the greater curvature approximately six centimeters from the pylorus. A loose sleeve is created over a forty French bougie, ensuring the incisura angularis is not impinged. The sleeve gastrectomy is completed using a green load staple line reinforcement, with careful removal of the fundus.

The surgeon stands between the patient's legs and mobilizes the first and second portions of the duodenum. The pylorus is identified, and the duodenum is mobilized both inferiorly and superiorly, with careful dissection in contact with the duodenum to avoid injury. Approximately four to five centimeters of the postpyloric duodenum are mobilized using antral traction. A plane is created just lateral to the common bile duct, and articulating sectors are used to create a retro duodenal tunnel. A pendular strain is applied to facilitate further duodenal mobilization.

The surgeon uses a blue load stapler with reinforcement to transect the fully mobilized duodenum, aided by the pendros strain for elevation. After transection, the surgeon moves to the left side of the patient to identify the ileocecal junction. The bowel is rotated in an anti-clockwise fashion, marking at 125 centimeters to signify the length of the common channel, and continues counting another 125 centimeters while maintaining the rotation for correct positioning.

The surgeon divides the bowel at 250 centimeters using a white load stapler, then divides the mesentery. A clip marks the biliary limb, which is rotated to align with the common channel. Enterotomies are created, and an anastomosis is fashioned between the biliary limb and the common channel.

The surgeon creates an H-shaped anastomosis using bidirectional firing with white load staplers to prevent narrowing of the distant, narrow-caliber bowel. The stapler is placed in the second direction, and the common enterotomy is closed transversely. The mesenteric defect is exposed by reflecting the small bowel upwards and closed with a running 2-0 silk stitch. The surgeon resumes position between the patient's legs and proceeds to take the Roux limb in an anti-colic fashion.

The surgeon creates a retrocolic tunnel to facilitate alignment of the small bowel with the post-pyloric duodenum. A stay suture is placed to secure the alignment, followed by a posterior running suture using a two-oh vicryl stitch, meticulously forming the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a tuovacral stitch to complete the posterior row of the anastomosis between the duodenum and ileum. The harmonic scalpel is employed to open both the ileum and post pyloric duodenum, sealing the mucosa to prevent eversion. A second layer of sutures is placed using a two ovicle stitch to ensure a wide anastomosis.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy with diamond configuration port placement for the procedure. Six trocars are used during the surgery. Sutures are run from superior to inferior to tie the anastomosis, followed by a second anterior layer to approximate the serosa.
The surgeon examines the completed anastomosis using an endoscope or methylene blue test. A drain is placed around the anastomosis, which will be removed on the third postoperative day.
